The equation is as follows:</p>\n\n<p class="mt-align-center">\\[\\text{Density}=\\dfrac{\\text{Mass}}{\\text{Volume}}\\]</p>\n\n<p>or just</p>\n\n<p class="mt-align-center">\\[\\rho =\\dfrac{m}{V} \\label{eq2}\\]</p>\n\n<p>Based on this equation, it\'s clear that density can, and does, vary from element to element and substance to substance due to differences in the relationship of mass and volume. Pure water, for example, has a density of 0.998 g/cm<sup>3</sup> at 25° C. The average densities of some common substances are in <span class="external">Table \\(\\PageIndex{1}\\).</span> Notice that corn oil has a lower mass to volume ratio than water. This means that when added to water, corn oil will “float.”</p>\n\n<table align="center" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0" class="mt-responsive-table">\n    <caption>Table \\(\\PageIndex{1}\\): Densities of Common Substances</caption>\n    <thead>\n        <tr>\n            <th scope="col" style="text-align:center;"><strong>Substance</strong></th>\n            <th scope="col" style="text-align:center;"><strong>Density at 25°C (g/cm3)</strong></th>\n        </tr>\n    </thead>\n    <tbody>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">blood</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">1.035</td>\n        </tr>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">body fat</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">0.918</td>\n        </tr>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">whole milk</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">1.030</td>\n        </tr>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">corn oil</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">0.922</td>\n        </tr>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">mayonnaise</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">0.910</td>\n        </tr>\n        <tr>\n            <td data-th="Substance" style="text-align:center;">honey</td>\n            <td data-th="Density at 25°C (g/cm3)" style="text-align:center;">1.420</td>\n        </tr>\n    </tbody>\n</table>\n\n<p>Density can be measured for all substances—solids, liquids and gases. For solids and liquids, density is often reported using the units of g/cm<sup>3</sup> or g/mL. Densities of gases, which are significantly lower than the densities of solids and liquids, are often given using units of g/L.</p>\n\n<div class="box-example">\n<div id="Density_as_a_Conversion_Factor"></span><h2 class="editable">Density as a Conversion Factor</h2>\n\n<p>Conversion factors can also be constructed for converting between different kinds of units. For example, density can be used to convert between the mass and the volume of a substance. Consider mercury, which is a liquid at room temperature and has a density of 13.6 g/mL. The density tells us that 13.6 g of mercury have a volume of 1 mL.
